title: Does TEE Solve AI Privacy?
content: What if I want to ask AI a legal question, a financial question, or a medical question? In practice, many people say things to AI chatbots they would never dare utter aloud, and maybe never have. This is about as close to giving Big Tech direct access to your brain as we’ve ever come. Even if one were to trust a given Big Tech company, would you feel comfortable publishing your chat history for anyone to read? At the time of writing, an AI platform has yet to experience a data breach (that we know of), but this is inevitable. Do you want a catalog of your thoughts read aloud in town square? The solution is to run AI models on your own hardware. The obstacle used to be that open-source models were not comparable to frontier Big Tech models, but this gap is closing rapidly. Kimi K2.6 is hot on the heels of Big Tech, and Kimi 3.0 is right around the corner. The obstacle is now solely cost. Running an AI server requires storage, memory, and compute that far exceeds the utility a typical person will derive from their AI, especially without 24/7 use. MapleAI (now Maple Research) promises a private way to interact with the best open-source AI models. Maple claims that only users can view their chat history, and that users can independently verify a server is running open-source code. The value proposition is simple and compelling. I give Maple money, Maple gives me efficient cloud compute for excellent AI models without being to spy on me. But is that true? Is it even possible? Let’s investigate. Chapter 1: Architecture Maple is open-source, so let’s start by looking at what components Maple uses and how they interact. The Maple codebase is the client-side application(s). This includes the web app, the iOS and android apps, and the various desktop apps. Opensecret is the server your client communicates with. Opensecret runs in the cloud, specfically on AWS Nitro EC2 instances. Those cloud containers are special, because they offer hardware based attestation, which is very important to this use case. Maple uses the Opensecret SDK to handle all of the communication between the Maple client and the Opensecret server. This is great, but neither Maple nor Opensecret have any AI in them… where’s my LLM? The problem is the Opensecret server runs on Nitro enclaves, which don’t have GPUs. The solution is a secondary TEE, running on a GPU, which can performantly run an AI inference engine like vLLM. At the time of writing, Opensecret implements two such providers: privatemode.ai and tinfoil.sh. This begs an interesting question, why use Opensecret at all? Why not access my hardware-attested secure inference engine running on a GPU TEE directly? The purpose of the Opensecret middleman comes from the limitations of hardware-attested cloud servers. CPU and GPU enclaves cannot perform the same work. For authentication, billing, session management, and database interactions, I need a CPU. For AI inference, I need a GPU. In order to get both, I must daisy-chain TEEs together into one system. In practice, my Maple client cryptographically verifies that an Opensecret server is running the open-source code I’ve inspected, while the Opensecret server verifies that my AI inference engine is running the open-source code I’ve inspected. Chapter 2: Sessions A session is a short-lived cryptographic relationship between a client and server. To understand Maple’s security guarantees, we have to start with how sessions are handled between the Maple client and the Opensecret enclave. Every session starts the same way: The client cryptographically proves it’s talking to a real enclave. The two sides negotiate a shared symmetric key. Every request/response body that could carry sensitive content is encrypted with this key before transmission. Login Flow When you launch Maple and log in, four things happen in order: attest, handshake, authenticate, store tokens. What makes this different from a normal HTTPS login is the first two steps. The client uses hardware attestation to prove that it’s not just talking to a server, but to a specific, known piece of code running inside an AWS-sealed box. First, the client demands a proof of identity from the server. The client generates a random UUID (128 bits, of which 122 are random) to use as a nonce that proves freshness. const attestationNonce = window.crypto.randomUUID(); const document = await verifyAttestation(attestationNonce, explicitApiUrl); This sends GET /attestation/{nonce} to the enclave. When the enclave receives this request, it creates a brand-new X25519 keypair for this session, then asks the AWS Nitro Secure Module (NSM) to produce an attestation document. The enclave is not responsible for signing this document, it hands the NSM drive three things to embed and AWS’ hardware key signs the result. let enclave_public_key = data.create_ephemeral_key(nonce.clone()).await; let request = Request::Attestation { user_data: None, public_key: Some(ByteBuf::from(enclave_public_key.as_bytes().to_vec())), nonce: Some(ByteBuf::from(nonce.into_bytes())), }; // abbreviated version of opensecret/src/web/attestation_routes.rs/generate_real_attestation(): let nsm_fd = nsm_init(); // open /dev/nsm let response = nsm_process_request(nsm_fd, request); // AWS hardware signs the document nsm_exit(nsm_fd); The server replies with an AWS Nitro attestation document: a blob containing a set of hash-chain measurements (the most important of which is called PCR0 and covers the whole enclave image), the nonce the client just sent, and an ephemeral X25519 public key newly-generated for this session. The client now needs to verify this proof. First, is the nonce correct? const documentNonce = new TextDecoder("utf-8").decode(document.nonce); if (nonce !== documentNonce) { throw new Error("Attestation document's nonce does not match the provided nonce."); } Next, is the certificate cryptographically valid? The client ships the AWS root CA as a compiled-in asset and refuses to proceed unelss the document’s root equals the one shipped with the client and the chain to the root CA is valid. if (encode(document.cabundle[0]) !== encode(trustedRootCert)) { throw new Error("Root cert does not match first cert in attestation document."); } const certChainItems = await chain.build(leafCert); // each cert must be signed by the next // ...notBefore/notAfter expiry check... if (certChainItems.length !== document.cabundle.length + 1) { throw new Error("Certificate chain length does not match length of cabundle."); } Next, the client rebuilds the exact byte structure that was signed and verifies the server’s signature over it. The system uses CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation; alternative to JSON) and COSE (CBOR Object Signing and Encryption; alternative to JWTs) because they offer deterministic encoding that enables reliable cryptographic verification. const signatureBytes = createSigStructure(documentData.protected, documentData.payload); const verified = await crypto.subtle.verify( { name: "ECDSA", hash: "SHA-384" }, publicKey, documentData.signature, signatureBytes ); if (!verified) throw new Error("Signature verification failed."); Now that the client has verified that the document is authentic, the last step in the proof is to verify that the PCR0 value matches a hard-coded allowlist or signed history file. if (validPcr0Values.includes(hash)) return { isMatch: true, text: "PCR0 matches a known good value" }; At this point, the trust chain looks like this: Enclave hands NSM: { user_data, nonce, ephemeral public_key } └─ AWS Nitro Secure Module builds an attestation document ... (CBOR map: PCRs, nonce, certs, public_key, ...) └─ wraps it as a COSE_Sign1 ... (CBOR array: [protected, {}, payload, signature]) └─ signs it with an AWS-hardware-rooted key └─ base64 over HTTP to the client └─ client: base64-decode → CBOR-decode COSE → verify cert chain to AWS root → verify signature → CBOR-decode payload → check PCRs + nonce The client has a cryptographic proof, rooted in AWS hardware, that it is talking to a specific known build of Opensecret inside a real Nitro enclave. Everything that follows can now safely rely on the X25519 public key included in the attestation document. Now the client begins a standard public/private keypair handshake using the trusted public key from the Nitro attestation document. The client generates its own X25519 keypair and posts the public half, plus the nonce, to /key_exchange. const response = await fetch(`${url}/key_exchange`, { method: "POST", headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" }, body: JSON.stringify({ client_public_key: clientPublicKey, nonce }) }); const { encrypted_session_key, session_id } = await response.json(); The server runs Diffie-Hellman over its ephemeral private key and the client’s public key to derive a shared secret. It then generates a fresh random 256-bit session key, encrypts that session key with the shared secret using ChaCha20-Poly1305, stores the session key server-side keyed by a session ID, and returns the encrypted blob to the client. let shared_secret = ephemeral_secret.diffie_hellman(&client_public_key); let session_key: [u8; 32] = crate::encrypt::generate_random(); let cipher = ChaCha20Poly1305::new(shared_secret.as_bytes().into()); let encrypted_session_key = cipher.encrypt(nonce, session_key.as_ref())?; data.session_states.write().await.insert(session_id, SessionState::new(session_key)); The client runs the matching Diffie-Hellman using the attested public key, which yields the identical shared secret, which decrypts the session key from the server. Both sides now hold the same 256-bit secret, and only a real, AWS-attested enclave could have produced it. const sharedSecret = nacl.scalarMult(clientKeyPair.secretKey, serverPublicKey); const chacha = new ChaCha20Poly1305(sharedSecret); const decryptedSessionKey = chacha.open(decryptionNonce, ciphertext); From this point onward, the remaining handshake is what we’d expect from a modern web application. The client encrypts the user’s password under the session key; the server decrypts it, verifies it against an Argon2 hash that is itself stored encrypted under the enclave key, then issues two JWTs: an access token and a refresh token. Every request body that could carry sensitive content (prompts, replies, credentials) is encrypted under the session key derived from the attested X25519 keypair, so it remains readable only by the enclave. This can be reviewed in OpenSecret-SDK/src/lib/encryption.ts, OpenSecret-SDK/src/lib/encryptedApi.ts, opensecret/src/web/encryption_middleware.rs, opensecret/src/web/login_routes.rs, opensecret/src/main.rs, and opensecret/src/models/users.rs. Inference Flow At this stage, we know that the client and the enclave have an end-to-end-encrypted session and communicate securely. I’ve logged in and now I want to prompt an AI model, how does that happen? When you send a prompt, Opensecret decrypts your prompt inside its enclave and somehow must get that plaintext to a GPU. A Nitro enclave has no GPU, so the prompt has to leave Opensecret for a second confidential machine, which we will call TEE2. Opensecret now does what our client did at login: it uses hardware attestation to prove TEE2 is a specific, known build of an inference server running on genuine confidential hardware. At the time of writing, Opensecret supports two possible TEE2 backends, Tinfoil and Privatemode. Opensecret chooses one when the enclave boots, defined by ProxyRouter and ProxyConfig: let default_proxy = ProxyConfig { base_url: openai_base.clone(), api_key: if openai_base.contains("api.openai.com") { openai_key } else { None }, provider_name: if openai_base.contains("api.openai.com") { "openai".to_string() } else { "continuum".to_string() }, }; let tinfoil_proxy = tinfoil_base .filter(|base| !base.is_empty()) .map(|base_url| ProxyConfig { base_url, api_key: None, provider_name: "tinfoil".to_string(), }); Self { default_proxy, tinfoil_proxy, } Wait, OpenAI? The above code sets Tinfoil as the default, but only if an environment variable is not set to api.openai.com. This was a big red flag when I first saw it. If I can just modify some environment variables and the code supports sending prompts to Big Tech, how do I know where my plaintext prompt is going? Furthermore, could it be connecting to any inference engine and still pass the source code hardware attestation? My best guess is that this route exists for development and testing reasons. However, we don’t have to trust that the server operator is using the correct environment variables. The enclave startup script, entrypoint.sh, controls these environment variables. This entrypoint file is part of the enclave image, and therefore is included in the PCR0 that our client uses at login to verify the code running on the Opensecret server. The OpenAI branch is unreachable in production; it survives only in the local-dev path, which emits a mock attestation no production client accepts. # start the privatemode proxy on 8092 run_forever continuum_proxy /app/continuum-proxy --port 8092 --apiKey "$continuum_proxy_api_key" & # set the openai environment variable to 8092 export OPENAI_API_BASE="http://127.0.0.1:8092" # start the tinfoil proxy on 8093 run_forever tinfoil_proxy env TINFOIL_API_KEY="$tinfoil_proxy_api_key" TINFOIL_PROXY_PORT=8093 /app/tinfoil-proxy & # set the tinfoil environment variable to 8093 export TINFOIL_API_BASE="http://127.0.0.1:8093" This confirms that any routing to OpenAI is being overwritten in the production environment, and our inference is happening on either Tinfoil or Privatemode. The only way to change this behavior is to change this script, and changing this script changes the PCR0, which our client would then reject. Both proxies run as separate processes within the Opensecret enclave. Opensecret talks to each proxy over plain HTTP on loopback; that is safe only because both processes live inside the same attested enclave. In the case of the Tinfoil proxy, verification is triggered when the proxy process starts and first constructs its HTTP client. client, err := tinfoil.NewClient(option.WithAPIKey(apiKey)) if err != nil { return nil, err } httpClient := *client.HTTPClient() // this line triggers verification HTTPClient() runs the full attestation once, caches the result, and from then on hands back a client whose transport is pinned to the attested TLS key. if s.groundTruth == nil { _, err := s.Verify() // full attestation, once if err != nil { return nil, fmt.Errorf("failed to verify enclave: %v", err) } } return &http.Client{ Transport: &TLSBoundRoundTripper{ExpectedPublicKey: s.groundTruth.TLSPublicKey}, }, nil Verify() establishes what should be running, what is running, then refuses to continue unless they match. // what SHOULD run: code measurement, proven by Sigstore digest, _ := github.FetchLatestDigest(s.repo) sigstoreBundle,_ := github.FetchAttestationBundle(s.repo, digest) codeMeasurement, err := sigstoreClient.VerifyAttestation(sigstoreBundle, s.repo, digest) // what IS running: live hardware attestation from the GPU box enclaveAttestation, _ := attestation.Fetch(s.enclave) // /.well-known/tinfoil-attestation enclaveVerification, err := enclaveAttestation.Verify() // cross-check: the hardware-measured code == the Sigstore-signed code if err = codeMeasurement.Equals(enclaveVerification.Measurement); err != nil { return nil, fmt.Errorf("measurements: %v", err) } The first part of this check is to establish what should be running. Sigstore is a public software-signing system. This is an alternative to the hard-coded allowlist that Maple<->Opensecret uses. Sigstore is automated while still being transparent and tamper-resistant. The Tinfoil proxy fetches the allowlist using Sigstore and GitHub. It is worth pausing on what the hardware attestation here actually is, because it is not the same mechanism as the AWS NSM attestation from the login flow, even though it plays the same role. The shape is shared: a hardware root of trust signs a document that says “here is a measurement of the code that booted, and here is some caller-chosen data I will bind to it,” and you verify by chaining the signature to a manufacturer root and checking the measurement against an expected value. What differs is the manufacturer, the measurement model, and how the caller-chosen data works. Tinfoil first fetches the document and learns the platform from it. The GPU box exposes an endpoint that returns a small JSON object whose Format field declares which silicon produced it. const ( SevGuestV2 PredicateType = "https://tinfoil.sh/predicate/sev-snp-guest/v2" TdxGuestV2 PredicateType = "https://tinfoil.sh/predicate/tdx-guest/v2" attestationEndpoint = "/.well-known/tinfoil-attestation" ) type Document struct { Format PredicateType `json:"format"` Body string `json:"body"` } Verify() switches on that declared format and runs the matching hardware verifier: switch d.Format { case SevGuestV2: return verifySevAttestationV2WithVCEK(d.Body, vcekDER) // AMD path case TdxGuestV2: return verifyTdxAttestationV2(d.Body) // Intel path default: return nil, fmt.Errorf("unsupported attestation format: %s", d.Format) } The self-declaration is not a trust hole, because the format only chooses which vendor PKI the signature is forced to satisfy. On the AMD path, the SEV-SNP report must verify against AMD’s chain. On the Intel path, the TDX quote must verify against Intel’s chain. A box that lies about its platform simply has to forge the other vendor’s signature instead, which it cannot do; and either way Tinfoil still cross-checks the measurement against the Sigstore-pinned expected value. The caller-chosen data is where this most differs from NSM. The login attestation passes an explicit nonce and public_key into the NSM request, so each session gets a fresh challenge. SEV-SNP and TDX instead expose a single fixed 64-byte report_data field that the guest fills once when it generates the report. The Tinfoil inference enclave puts its TLS public key there, and the verifier reads it back out of the hardware-signed report. // the SEV-SNP report's 64-byte ReportData carries the enclave's keys return newVerificationV2(measurement, report.ReportData), nil // ... TLSPublicKeyFP: hex.EncodeToString(keys[:32]), // first 32 bytes = TLS pubkey {: file=”tinfoil-go/verifier/attestation/{sev.go:170, attestation.go:98-104}” } The verifier then opens a TLS connection to the enclave and refuses it unless the live certificate’s public key equals the one the hardware signed into report_data. certFP, err := attestation.ConnectionCertFP(conn.ConnectionState()) if certFP != enclaveVerification.TLSPublicKeyFP { return fmt.Errorf("certificate fingerprint mismatch: expected %s, got %s", enclaveVerification.TLSPublicKeyFP, certFP) } This is the mechanical reason the inference path is “attest once, pin the key” rather than “fresh nonce per request” like login: the hardware report binds a long-lived TLS identity, not a per-call challenge, so the strong guarantee is delivered by pinning every subsequent connection to that attested key rather than by re-attesting each prompt. Now Opensecret sends the plaintext prompt to the local Tinfoil proxy as an ordinary OpenAI-format POST. This hop is plain HTTP on loopback inside the enclave; the attested TLS key secures the next hop, when the proxy forwards it out to TEE2. let mut req = Request::builder() .method("POST") .uri(format!("{}/v1/chat/completions", proxy_config.base_url)) .header("Content-Type", "application/json"); // ... let req = req.body(HyperBody::from(body_json.to_string()))?; // plaintext body The Tinfoil proxy is responsible for getting this plaintext prompt off the Nitro box, and it does so only over the vetted connection. It uses a Tinfoil API key decrypted in entrypoint.sh. func (s *proxyServer) upstreamURL(path, rawQuery string) string { upstream := url.URL{Scheme: "https", Host: s.enclaveHost, Path: path, RawQuery: rawQuery} return upstream.String() } // ... req, _ := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, c.Request.Method, s.upstreamURL(path, c.Request.URL.RawQuery), c.Request.Body) copyHeaders(req.Header, c.Request.Header) req.Header.Set("Authorization", "Bearer "+s.apiKey) req.Host = s.enclaveHost resp, err := doWithResponseStartTimeout(s.httpClient, req, upstreamResponseStartTimeout) So we can trust that our prompt reaches the Tinfoil inference engine securely and that Opensecret will only send it to a Tinfoil server that is running the expected public source code, but what does Tinfoil do with our prompt once it gets there? Tinfoil publishes their server-side code in the cvimage repository. The pinned TLS connection terminates inside the confidential VM and is handled by the tinfoil-shim service. The shim is an OpenAI-compatible reverse proxy: it reads the chat request and forwards it to the model. func (t *streamTransport) RoundTrip(req *http.Request) (*http.Response, error) { if req.URL.Path != "/v1/chat/completions" { return t.base.RoundTrip(req) } // ...read body, then forward to the model: resp, err := t.base.RoundTrip(req) The models run as a separate container, and the container name (config.UpstreamContainer) and image digest are baked into the attested tinfoil-config.yml. At startup, the shim asks Docker for that named container’s IP on a private, in-VM-only network (shim-net). The upstream port comes from shim.upstream-port in the same attested config, so while the IP is resolved at runtime, the identity of the destination is fixed by the measured config. // at startup, resolve the attested container name to its IP on shim-net upstreamHost, _ := resolveUpstreamHost(ctx, config.UpstreamContainer) upstreamAddr := fmt.Sprintf("%s:%d", upstreamHost, config.UpstreamPort) // every chat request is rewritten to that fixed upstream req.URL.Scheme = "http" req.URL.Host = upstreamAddr req.Header.Set("Host", "localhost") {: file=”cvmimage/tinfoil/cmd/shim/{main.go:207-211, api.go:146-149}” } The final destination of our prompt is vLLM. At boot, the Tinfoil server verifies which version of vLLM is included in the server image. vLLM is specified in a config file, and that config’s SHA-256 is carried on the kernel command line, which is part of the same measured boot covered by AMD or Intel hardware attestation. To change the engine, you must change the config and change the config’s hash, which changes the measured hardware attested value. On top of that, model weights get a second dm-verity integrity check. If vLLM sees our plaintext request (which it must), then our trust chain can be broken at the last step if vLLM is logging it or sending it off to a third-party. vLLM’s logging and usage-statistics behavior is controlled by its launch arguments, and those arguments live in tinfoil-config.yml, whose SHA-256 is carried on the measured kernel command line, so the exact vLLM invocation is measured by hardware attestation. The other structural guarantee is that the confidential VM boots a read-only, integrity-checked root filesystem: the kernel command line carries readonly=on and a dm-verity roothash=, both folded into the same SEV-SNP/TDX launch measurement, so vLLM cannot persist a prompt to disk or silently run modified code. Storing Chats By default, Maple stores your conversation history server-side so you can return to it from any device. This sounds like it should torpedo the privacy story, but the schema is built around the same heuristic as the rest of the system: every column that contains user content is stored as ciphertext, and the only key that can decrypt it lives inside the enclave. The database schema for chat content uses column names suffixed _enc: prompt_enc — the prompts you sent content_enc — message bodies name_enc — conversation titles metadata_enc — anything else attached to a response attachment_text_enc — any file/image text you’ve uploaded When a row is read, the enclave runs decrypt_content(&user_key, msg.content_enc.as_ref()), where user_key is a per-user secret derived inside the enclave from material that never leaves the enclave. If anyone outside the enclave were to dump the database, they would see a row of opaque bytes and nothing else. Even the row count and approximate timing leak only metadata, not content. pub fn decrypt_content<T>( key: &SecretKey, encrypted: Option<&Vec<u8>>, ) -> Result<Option<T>, EncryptError> where T: DeserializeOwned { let Some(encrypted) = encrypted else { return Ok(None); }; let decrypted_bytes = decrypt_with_key(key, encrypted)?; let value = serde_json::from_slice(&decrypted_bytes) .map_err(|e| EncryptError::DeserializationFailed(e.to_string()))?; Ok(Some(value)) } This is the same trust chain we’ve been tracing extended one more hop. The database is not in the enclave, so the database doesn’t get to see plaintext. The enclave acts as the gatekeeper, decrypting on read and encrypting on write, and the keys live in measured-and-attested memory. Chapter 3: Proofs We can inspect the code for all parts of this system. When we generate a plaintext prompt, we can perfectly trace its visibility and flow through the architecture. At every point, the next destination of a prompt or reply is deterministic. This observation offers strong security and privacy guarantees, but they rest on the ability of end users to compare the hashes the source code checks with the hashes of the codebase when they build it themselves. The system does emit one outbound stream: HMAC-signed billing events containing token counts, model name, and a request ID, never prompts or replies, to a destination fixed in the attested tinfoil-config.yml and constrained by an attested nftables egress allowlist. Reproducible Builds In order to confirm that the system is running the code in the codebases we inspected, we need to find every point where a hash is checked and compare the “good” value with a value we arrived at ourselves. If I build the code on my laptop and the hashes don’t match the ones the system is using, I have no idea what code is actually running. So let’s enumerate every hash the system checks, and ask, for each one: can I, on my own machine, arrive at the same value? There are really only two numbers that matter, because everything else folds into them. The Maple client checks one measurement: the PCR0 of the Opensecret enclave. The Opensecret enclave checks one measurement: the SEV-SNP (or TDX) launch measurement of the Tinfoil model box. Every other hash we’ve seen is an input to one of those two: Hash the system checks What it covers Folds into PCR0 The whole Opensecret enclave image: the Rust binary, entrypoint.sh, nitro-bins, the continuum and tinfoil proxies TEE1 measurement SEV-SNP / TDX launch measurement The confidential VM: kernel, initrd, OVMF firmware, read-only root filesystem TEE2 measurement tinfoil-config.yml SHA-256 The vLLM image digest and its launch flags; placed on the kernel command line TEE2 measurement cvmimage kernel/initrd SHA-256, rootfs dm-verity roothash The confidential VM’s contents TEE2 measurement Model-weights dm-verity root hash (the mpk) The model weights, integrity-checked separately at mount TEE2 (via the attested config) If I can independently reproduce the two measurements at the bottom of the “folds into” funnel, every input above them is verified for free, because changing any input changes the measurement. The Scripts Doing a one-time reproduction is a significant and valuable result, but knowing that someone is honest right now proves nothing about whether they will be honest tomorrow. Therefore, I wanted to automate all the work I do for reproduction, and make it as hands-off as possible. My goal is that any auditor or user should be able to download a script, run it, and check for themselves whether Maple is being honest. The script should do the absolute maximum possible configuration for you, only requiring user intervention when unavoidable. Friction is bad. It only took me a few hours to get a working version of a script that successfully checked and installed dependencies, cloned all of the source material, configured Nix, fetched the attestation and AWS certificates, and built the enclave images from source. However, the PCR0s did not match. That’s the whole point! No need to break out the pitchforks though, if your first attempt works you’re more likely to have done something wrong than done something right. To ensure that my build procedure was correct, I began with a commit dated a while ago. This allows me to check my process, and protects me from a push-before-we-update-the-attestation situtation in the commit history. For a while, I was getting PCR0s that didn’t exist anywhere in the Openscret repository. What really killed me was build speed. The Opensecret enclave image is built for ARM Linux because production runs on AWS Graviton (aarch64) Nitro instances. I only have x86 hardware, so the build has to happen under emulation to produce the same bytes the live system is running. Opensecret is built with Nix, which has built-in support for QEMU emulation, but emulated builds are slow. Every tweak or attempt to correct any build issues was therefore followed by several hours of emulated Nix builds, even with caching. After much tweaking and trial-and-error, I finally dialed in my OS and tools, and produced a PCR0 of d4218d625c17a7bbff6dd95c8ccea98d851284744c43cfbae98e86a7d7d504c1dc80908a6d34eab6f859262502db3366 from commit 416c03e. This is a deprecated PCR0, but it was used as a production attestation in the past. This proved that my approach and configuration were correct, and also proved that Maple was using the code they claim to use, and that Opensecret is byte-reproducible from source. But I’m a stubborn fella, and I want the live PCR0. I don’t care what Maple was running yesterday, I care what is running right now. Maple does not use releases for Opensecret, so the closest thing we have to documentation about what PCR0 corresponds to what commit is the commits that change the PCR0 values themselves. We know that the live production PCR0 is listed in pcrprod.json, and we can see when this value changes via the commit history on that file. Today is 2026-05-31. The current live PCR0 is 3c374a4b3c4f4cd5c5a6cc28d89cc2462fdd129eac5cd0eacf7fc78b98af11e9a08750a76cd1c1ab75c2715300530a26. This value was introduced at commit b2eee16 on 2026-05-06 and the previous PCR0 of d4218d625c17a7bbff6dd95c8ccea98d851284744c43cfbae98e86a7d7d504c1dc80908a6d34eab6f859262502db3366 was deprecated. By checking out to b2eee16, the same script and environment that produced the previous matching PCR0 produced 3c374a4b3c4f4cd5c5a6cc28d89cc2462fdd129eac5cd0eacf7fc78b98af11e9a08750a76cd1c1ab75c2715300530a26, which is the live production attestation! Victory! However, is this victory certain in the future? I succeeded because the commit that updated pcrProd.json built to the live PCR0, but this may not always be the case. Opensecret does not use releases. Therefore, there is no clear delineation of what to build. The Opensecret codebase is updated regularly, but not every GitHub commit affects the PCR0 measurement. Roughly, a commit changes PCR0 only if it changes the inputs to the EIF derivation: the Rust source under src/, Cargo.lock, rust-toolchain.toml, flake.nix (when the kernel config or build pipeline change), entrypoint.sh, or the committed binaries under nitro-bins/, continuum-proxy, and tinfoil-proxy/dist/. Commits that touch only prcProd.json, pcrProdHistory.json, documentation, CI workflows, tests, or the README do not change the PCR0, even though they advance the master branch. This is why two adjacent commits can have identical PCR0s and a long stretch of main can share a single deployed measurement. Therefore, I included a walk-back mechanism in my script which looks for the live attestation PCR0 and, if the first candiate commit fails, works around it to try and find a commit that builds to the expected PCR0. The tools I used are available at wtthoma4/maple-tee-audit. I will do my best to keep them fresh. Opensecret Results I reproduced two production PCR0s from source, on two different commits, using only public code: GitHub Commit Status (time of writing) Built PCR0 b2eee16 Live production PCR0 3c374a4b3c4f4cd5c5a6cc28d89cc2462fdd129eac5cd0eacf7fc78b98af11e9a08750a76cd1c1ab75c2715300530a26 416c03e Deprecated production PCR0 d4218d625c17a7bbff6dd95c8ccea98d851284744c43cfbae98e86a7d7d504c1dc80908a6d34eab6f859262502db3366 Together these prove both halves of the claim: the system is reproducible from public source today, and it was reproducible from public source for at least one prior deploy. The live attestation document also passed the full cryptographic chain; its certificate validates to the AWS Nitro root, its COSE_Sign1 signature is valid, and the nonce I generated in the run was echoed back inside the signed payload, so I know the attestation is fresh and not replayed. There is one documented gap, in nitro-bins/: two AWS-derived prebuilt binaries (kmstool_enclave_cli, libnsm.so) ship as committed bytes in the Opensecret repo. AWS’s published recipe for rebuilding them has a transitive dependency that now requires a newer Rust edition than the recipe pins, so the recipe doesn’t build today. This doesn’t weaken the trust chain. Those bytes are committed and folded into PCR0, so my reproduced PCR0 covers them exactly it just means I can’t independently rebuild them. Notably, OpenSecret has already started addressing this: PR #191 (titled “Restore reproducible nitro-bins build”) is merged into main at the time of writing. Tinfoil Results For each of the two confidential VMs I depend on, I independently recomputed the SEV-SNP launch measurement and compared it to Tinfoil’s signed value: Component Version Recomputed SEV-SNP measurement Matches Sigstore-signed? confidential-model-router v0.0.104 b126c633a662ee13b77c8ff21cb29b9ac2da92674d290298a52b259fc74f6e9ed4691ffb240587834777a52d24e19f69 Yes confidential-kimi-k2-6 v0.0.3 8b7efee8da8103e718e900b60d4cc769282fe19fd793cb0a44a7fd5bae0bc631e180eea3a9dc06ad2b2777ffb6a9215e Yes The recomputation was done with the upstream sev-snp-measure tool maintained by AMD’s VirTEE project. The inputs to the recomputation are the published cvmimage vmlinuz, initrd, OVMF firmware, and tinfoil-config.yml for each model; the SHA-256s of the kernel and initrd were verified against the cvmimage release manifest, and the manifest’s provenance was verified via gh attestation verify against Sigstore and its Rekor transparency log. The live model enclave at kimi-k2-6.inf13.tinfoil.sh was also reachable and serving a real attestation document. The notable limitation on the Tinfoil side is that I have not yet rebuilt the cvmimage itself from source. Sigstore + Rekor is meaningful trust, it ties each published artifact to a short-lived signing certificate bound to the identity of the GitHub-hosted runner attached to the tinfoilsh/cvmimage repository, and the signature is recorded in an append-only public log. This is a notch weaker than building from source. I attempted the rebuild and ran into a few recipe-portability issues on Tinfoil’s side (notably, undeclared build-tool dependencies and package-version conflicts when installing from the pinned Ubuntu snapshot); none of them are security gaps in the running system.

title: Bitcoin via TrueNAS Virtualization
tags: TrueNAS, Open Source, Bitcoin, GitHub, VMs, Debian, Linux, ZFS
content: I need a Bitcoin node. I have a TrueNAS server. Let’s figure it out! Motivation I am working on a project that requires extensive interactions with the Bitcoin mainnet, testnet, and a mempool. I need a box in my development environment and a standardized build procedure. I decided to document the whole process! Available Hardware I have a TrueNAS server with plenty of CPU and RAM headroom to take on this project. The sketchy area is disk space. I bought a 1TB SSD for this project, and that will suffice for now, but within a few years a 1TB drive will no longer hold the unpruned blockchain plus the chainstate data. ZFS doesn’t like single-drive pools, and also doesn’t like full drives, and the current blockchain size will be cutting it close. As the blockchain grows, I expect the pool’s performance to worsen. I could buy another drive to set up a mirror, but storage is expensive and I don’t need two copies of the blockchain. If the SSD explodes tomorrow, I can follow this guide to recover my environment and sync the blockchain from scratch. When the size of the blockchain finally exceeds my 1TB SSD, the cheapest solution will be to extend the pool across a second 1TB drive. This is not an ideal setup, but all great tech spaghetti begins with yesterday’s budget consciousness, so at least I’ll have something to write an article about in the future! Why Not Docker? TrueNAS Scale has excellent Docker support, and I could use the community docker-bitcoind image https://apps.truenas.com/catalog/bitcoind/. There are a few downsides to using Docker. First, iXSystems has pushed painful, breaking updates to the TrueNAS apps ecosystem in the past. This is not as troublesome for stateless microservices and simple web applications, but it could be a serious issue for a monolith like bitcoind. Additionally, VM security and isolation is simply superior to Docker security and isolation. Bitcoin nodes are quite noisy and reach out to many third parties, by design, and containing this exposure within a VM is an appropriate isolation measure. Rather than configuring TrueNAS permissions for the dataset, running application, network stack, etc., we can just give bitcoind its own file system and users. Furthermore, because we can use UEFI + virtio, our VM will be vendor-agnostic and can be easily ported to Proxmox or VMWare in the future. Lastly, Docker apps on TrueNAS Scale route all disk I/O through ZFS, which is a huge performance hit for large sequential writes (like the initial block download). By using a VM on a ZVOL (raw block device), we can write these massive amounts of data without the copy-on-write ZFS overhead. This also avoids the file-level permissioning nightmare that commonly impacts TrueNAS Docker applications and are difficult to troubleshoot. Creating the VM First, create a ZFS pool so we have a place to store out VM and our data. Your hardware might be different, so differences at this stage can be safely ignored. Make sure you have enough storage for the current (and near-term future) size of the blockchain and the chainstate data. The result of my pool configuration is below: Step two is to select an operating system for our purpose and download it. I chose Debian NetInst because it’s lightweight (low disk usage by the OS itself and low RAM usage at idle, ~400MB). You can also install NetInst without a GUI, which is unnecessary for this project and helps a lot on the resource side. To download, visit Debian’s download page https://cdimage.debian.org/debian-cd/current/amd64/iso-cd/. Download the SHA256SUMS and debian-<version>-amd64-netinst.iso files. Take the extra security step to verify the integrity of the file. This isn’t foolproof, but it’s a good practice and something we’ll repeat more in-depth later. I downloaded version 13.3.0. On windows: $ Get-FileHash .\debian-13.3.0-amd64-netinst.iso Algorithm Hash --------- ---- SHA256 C9F09D24B7E834E6834F2FFA565B33D6F1F540D04BD25C79AD9953BC79A8AC02 Compare that SHA256 value with the corresponding value in the SHA256SUMS file. For 13.3.0, it was: c9f09d24b7e834e6834f2ffa565b33d6f1f540d04bd25c79ad9953bc79a8ac02 debian-13.3.0-amd64-netinst.iso So we have a match! Step three is to spin up the VM in the TrueNAS Scale web interface. In the sidebar, navigate to Virtualization. In the top-right corner, click the blue Add button. This will open a dialogue bar on the right-hand side of the screen which walks you through creating the VM. Change the Guest Operating System to Linux. Set the Name to whatever you want. Pick a good password. Everything else can stay as default. See my configuration below: Next, we configure the resources we’re giving the VM. We need to distinguish between two phases of the Bitcoin node lifescycle: IBD (initial block download) and normal operation. During IBD, the node will consume as much CPU, RAM, I/O, and network as you give it, until it reaches a bottleneck somewhere. I most often see bottlenecks in CPU or disk write speed. During normal operation, your node will consume hardly anything, it’s very lightweight. The IBD can sometimes take north of a week if the node has a bottleneck somewhere. To achieve a faster IBD, you can increase the CPU resources, specifically Cores (the IBD does not benefit greatly from hyperthreaded cores aka Threads or virtual sockets aka vCPUs, it needs bare metal cores). If you choose to speed up the IBD by adding more Cores, make sure you reduce this back to one after the IBD is complete. I gave my node one Core, Thread, and vCPU. I gave my node 4 GiB of RAM and set a minimum (reserved) RAM pool at 2 GiB (that guarantees at least 2 GiB will be available and TrueNAS will not choke the VM). See my configuration below: Next, we’re going to assign the disk to bitcoin-pool (or whatever you named your pool) and set the size to 50 GiB. Make sure Create a new disk image is the selected radial option and Select Disk Type is set to VirtIO. See my configuration below: Do not set the size to the whole disk size! Start with a small pool size to accomodate only the guest OS. Doing so will make the file system configuration much easier later. Next, we need to tell TrueNAS how to give our VM network access. Set the Adapter Type to VirtIO, leave the MAC Address to the auto-generated one, and pick your host ethernet adapter interface from the Attach NIC dropdown list (you most likely will only have one option to choose). See my configuration below: Next, we need to give TrueNAS the installation media for our operating system of choice. Click the Choose File upload button and navigate to the iso file you downloaded from Debian. This upload may take some time to complete, and the web interface may appear to be hung. Do not refresh or click back! You will have to repeat all the steps. I highly encourage you to use the built-in upload button on the dialogue via the TrueNAS Scale web interface rather than trying to move the iso onto a network share. Even if you use the TrueNAS shell to move the file from the share onto an internal directory, you still have to reconfigure the permissions for all different processes and utilities that need to touch it during the VM creation process. TrueNAS will do all of this for you if you just upload the iso directly. You can leave the GPU section unchanged (empty): You should now see your VM in the list of available machines! If you click the dropdown arrow, you should see the configuration . Double check the CPU, and RAM. If the VM is not already started, click the Start button. Once the VM is running, click the Display button: Configuring Debian Initial Set Up We will use TrueNAS’ built-in Spice display for the initial GUI set up. When you first open it (by clicking the Display button in the VM list in the TrueNAS web interface), you will put in the password you submitted earlier in the TrueNAS VM set up. After that, you should see this: Use your keyboard to select Install. On the next screen, select English - English: Next, pick your locale: Next, pick your keyboard layout: Next, pick your hostname. I chose debian: When asked about your domain name, you can safely leave this blank and continue: Next, pick a good root password and confirm it: Next, pick a username and password for yourself. This is how you will interact with the system. This user will have full control of the system as well as remote login capabilities, so pick a good password and confirm it: Next, pick your time zone: Now, we need to configure our disks. This is where the initial VM size of ~50 GiB comes in very handy. We can instruct the wizard to just use the entire disk, we don’t need to try and configure partitions here. Select vda (should be the only disk you can select), choose All files in one partition for the scheme, then Finish partitioning and write changes to the disk: Confirm and choose yes: When prompted to scan for extra installation media, choose no: Choose the location closest to you for best package manager performance. If you’re lucky enough to have a local mirror, choose that one, otherwise, the default deb.debian.org is fine: Leave the proxy configuration blank and continue: Choose no when prompted for voluntary data collection: When prompted for which software to install, play close attention. Unselect the GNOME (or any selected entry under Debian desktop environment) option using the spacebar. The only two options selected should be SSH server and standard system utilities: On the final page, choose continue. The system will not boot yet. After the reboot, you will go through the same prompts again. This is normal! See the next section for further instructions. Configuring Boot and Disks After the initial set up, we need to remove the iso to get our VM to boot into the actual OS and not the installation media. Navigate to the TrueNAS Virtualization page and click the Devices button on your VM. You will see four entries. Click the three dots on the right-hand side corresponding to the CD-ROM entry (which should also have Order of 1000) and click Delete: Click Delete Device: Now, reboot the VM. After the VM has rebooted, click the Display button. You should now see the Debian login screen instead of the set up menus: We now have a working Debian VM! Shut it down so we can make a few changes. Next, we need to give our VM access to the remainder of the storage space from our pool. Navigate to TrueNAS > Datasets and find the pool we created earlier. The pool should have one entry: a ZVOL with the name of your VM as the prefix. Click on the ZVOL then click Edit Zvol. Change the Size for this Zvol to the maximum that you can. For me that was 880 GiB. Check the Force size box and click Save: Power the VM back on. Now it’s time to SSH into the VM. Depending on how your network is configured, you may be able to do this using the hostname you configured in the initial set up. Alternatively, you may need to log in to your router/firewall/switch and identify the IP address your VM has been assigned by DHCP. $ ssh username@<hostname|ip> username@<hostname|ip> password: Linux debian 6.12.73+deb13-amd64 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Debian 6.12.73-1 (2026-02-17) x86_64 The programs included with the Debian GNU/Linux system are free software; the exact distribution terms for each program are described in the individual files in /usr/share/doc/*/copyright. Debian GNU/Linux comes with ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY, to the extent permitted by applicable law. Last login: Sun Mar 8 11:40:01 2026 from 192.168.1.147 username@<hostname|ip>:~$ For the rest of this article, you will see taylor@debian because that is the username and hostname I chose. So now our VM should have access to the whole drive. We can check this with the lsblk command: taylor@debian:~$ lsblk NAME MAJ:MIN RM SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINTS vda 254:0 0 840G 0 disk ├─vda1 254:1 0 976M 0 part /boot/efi ├─vda2 254:2 0 46.5G 0 part / └─vda3 254:3 0 2.6G 0 part [SWAP] We can see that the vda drive now has 840G of space, but only about 50GiB is allocated. This is ideal, because now we can cleanly assign the remaining space for Bitcoin Core data. First, create a new partition to use the new space using the sudo fdisk /dev/vda command. taylor@debian:~$ sudo fdisk /dev/vda Welcome to fdisk (util-linux 2.41). Changes will remain in memory only, until you decide to write them. Be careful before using the write command. GPT PMBR size mismatch (104857599 != 1761607679) will be corrected by write. The backup GPT table is not on the end of the device. This problem will be corrected by write. This disk is currently in use - repartitioning is probably a bad idea. It's recommended to umount all file systems, and swapoff all swap partitions on this disk. ... When prompted for the command, input n then put in a partition number of 4 (1-3 are already occupied): Command (m for help): n Partition number (4-128, default 4): 4 First sector (104855552-1761607646, default 104855552): Last sector, +/-sectors or +/-size{K,M,G,T,P} (104855552-1761607646, default 1761605631): Created a new partition 4 of type 'Linux filesystem' and of size 790 GiB. ... When prompted for your next command, input w. This will write your new partition to the disk. fdisk will exit back to the shell when this is done: Command (m for help): w The partition table has been altered. Syncing disks. taylor@debian:~$ Next let’s verify that vda4 was created as expected. Use the lsblk command again: taylor@debian:~$ lsblk NAME MAJ:MIN RM SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINTS vda 254:0 0 840G 0 disk ├─vda1 254:1 0 976M 0 part /boot/efi ├─vda2 254:2 0 46.5G 0 part / ├─vda3 254:3 0 2.6G 0 part [SWAP] └─vda4 254:4 0 790G 0 part We can see that vda took up the 790G of unallocated new space. Perfect. Next, we need to format the partition and create a mount point we can give to our Bitcoin Core node. Run sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/vda4 to format the new space: taylor@debian:~$ sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/vda4 mke2fs 1.47.2 (1-Jan-2025) Discarding device blocks: done Creating filesystem with 207093760 4k blocks and 51773440 inodes Filesystem UUID: f7461174-1a26-4590-a1d0-6f2b2d63189f Superblock backups stored on blocks: 32768, 98304, 163840, 229376, 294912, 819200, 884736, 1605632, 2654208, 4096000, 7962624, 11239424, 20480000, 23887872, 71663616, 78675968, 102400000 Allocating group tables: done Writing inode tables: done Creating journal (262144 blocks): done Writing superblocks and filesystem accounting information: done Run sudo mkdir -p /mnt/bitcoin to set up the destination folder: taylor@debian:~$ sudo mkdir -p /mnt/bitcoin Now, to create the actual mount point, we need to get the internal ID of the vda4 partition. Technically you can use the device name of /dev/vda4, but these assignments are at the mercy of the OS and might change. For a truly stable mount, we need to use the UUID. To get it, run sudo blkid /dev/vda4: taylor@debian:~$ sudo blkid /dev/vda4 /dev/vda4: UUID="f7461174-1a26-4590-a1d0-6f2b2d63189f" BLOCK_SIZE="4096" TYPE="ext4" PARTUUID="2e96b7ee-c584-463a-9fe1-b2a845b93811" Now that we have the UUID f7461174-1a26-4590-a1d0-6f2b2d63189f, we need to edit the /etc/fstab file. We will use a text editor called Nano to make these this small change. The fstab file (filesystem table) tells linux when, where, and how to create mount points to the underlying file system. In order to access our newly formatted partition via a path name like /mnt/bitcoin, we need to make sure linux will reliably create the link between the two. taylor@debian:~$ sudo nano /etc/fstab Once you’re inside Nano editing the /etc/fstab file, scroll to the bottom and add a new entry. I recommend leaving a comment above by using the pound symbol # and saying when and why you created this mount point. See the template below, you can copy it excplicitly and change the UUID: # new mount point for /mnt/bitcoin -taylor 2026-03-01 UUID=f7461174-1a26-4590-a1d0-6f2b2d63189f /mnt/bitcoin ext4 defaults,noatime,nofail 0 2 When you’re finished, hit Ctrl + X then Y to exit out of Nano while saving your changes. Let’s check success. Use sudo mount -a to force Linux to recreate all the mount points, then df -h /mnt/bitcoin to see the status of the new mount we just created: taylor@debian:~$ sudo mount -a taylor@debian:~$ df -h /mnt/bitcoin Filesystem Size Used Avail Use% Mounted on /dev/vda4 777G 2.1M 738G 1% /mnt/bitcoin Great! Now we have all the prerequisite work completed. We can proceed to installing Bitcoin Core. Installing and Configuring Bitcoin Core Before we begin, what does it mean to “Download Bitcoin”? There are competing forks of Bitcoin and competing versions (0.1 through 30 at the time of writing), so which one should you pick? If you want your node to support the critical soft forks that are active today, SegWit and Taproot, then you need at least v22.0. (SegWit support was introduced much earlier, but Taproot enforcement was first fully implemented in v22.0.). However, Bitcoin version updates aren’t for fun, each one brings important security, reliability, or performance upgrades. So, the latest version? Well, many Bitcoiners are not happy with the way changes were made for the most recent release, Bitcoin Core v30. A controversial change was made despite significant pushback (you can read about OP_RETURN and make up your own mind), which spawned the competing fork known as Bitcoin Knots. The answer to this question has to come from your reason to do this in the first place. I need a node to work on software that will be talking to a node, and if I want that software to be useful to others, I need to make sure it’s compatible with newer versions. For this reason, I am downloading v28, which was released in October 2024. The instructions that follow should be largely version-agnostic. To download the files we need, we’re going to use the wget command. To use it, we need the exact URL of the file. I navigated to https://bitcoincore.org/bin/bitcoin-core-28.0/ and copied the download links to the x86 64-bit linux archive (the .tar.gz), the SHA256SUMS, and the SHA256SUMS.asc files. Use wget to download them: taylor@debian:~$ wget https://bitcoincore.org/bin/bitcoin-core-28.0/bitcoin-28.0-x86_64-linux-gnu.tar.gz --2026-03-01 12:53:19-- https://bitcoincore.org/bin/bitcoin-core-28.0/bitcoin-28.0-x86_64-linux-gnu.tar.gz Resolving bitcoincore.org (bitcoincore.org)... 198.251.83.116, 208.68.7.208, 2620:6e:a004:1::b1c:c05e Connecting to bitcoincore.org (bitcoincore.org)|198.251.83.116|:443... connected. HTTP request sent, awaiting response... 200 OK Length: 49598317 (47M) [application/gzip] Saving to: ‘bitcoin-28.0-x86_64-linux-gnu.tar.gz’ bitcoin-28.0-x86_64-linux-gnu.tar.gz 100%[===================================>] 47.30M 14.2MB/s in 3.3s 2026-03-01 12:53:23 (14.2 MB/s) - ‘bitcoin-28.0-x86_64-linux-gnu.tar.gz’ saved [49598317/49598317] taylor@debian:~$ wget https://bitcoincore.org/bin/bitcoin-core-28.0/SHA256SUMS.asc --2026-03-01 12:54:25-- https://bitcoincore.org/bin/bitcoin-core-28.0/SHA256SUMS.asc Resolving bitcoincore.org (bitcoincore.org)... 198.251.83.116, 208.68.7.208, 2620:6e:a004:1::b1c:c05e Connecting to bitcoincore.org (bitcoincore.org)|198.251.83.116|:443... connected. HTTP request sent, awaiting response... 200 OK Length: 9109 (8.9K) [text/plain] Saving to: ‘SHA256SUMS.asc’ SHA256SUMS.asc 100%[===================================>] 8.90K --.-KB/s in 0s 2026-03-01 12:54:25 (341 MB/s) - ‘SHA256SUMS.asc’ saved [9109/9109] taylor@debian:~$ wget https://bitcoincore.org/bin/bitcoin-core-28.0/SHA256SUMS --2026-03-01 12:57:14-- https://bitcoincore.org/bin/bitcoin-core-28.0/SHA256SUMS Resolving bitcoincore.org (bitcoincore.org)... 198.251.83.116, 208.68.7.208, 2620:6e:a004:1::b1c:c05e Connecting to bitcoincore.org (bitcoincore.org)|198.251.83.116|:443... connected. HTTP request sent, awaiting response... 200 OK Length: 2620 (2.6K) [application/octet-stream] Saving to: ‘SHA256SUMS’ SHA256SUMS 100%[===================================>] 2.56K --.-KB/s in 0s 2026-03-01 12:57:14 (94.6 MB/s) - ‘SHA256SUMS’ saved [2620/2620] Before we unpack the archive files, we need to verify that they haven’t been tampered with. The basic idea here is that we can hash the archive we downloaded and compare it with the hash that the developers have provided. This is an excellent checking mechanism, but if an attacker managed to tamper with the archive file, they could easily just re-compute the hash then tamper with the hash file as well. So how do we make sure the hash file hasn’t been tampered with? The developers also provide a digitally signed version of the SHA256SUMS file. Anyone can hash, but only the developers can sign. But how do we check the signatures? This is step one. From the public GitHub, download the manifest of developer public keys: taylor@debian:~$ wget https://github.com/bitcoin-core/guix.sigs/archive/refs/heads/master.tar.gz --2026-03-01 13:06:21-- https://github.com/bitcoin-core/guix.sigs/archive/refs/heads/master.tar.gz Resolving github.com (github.com)... 140.82.112.4 Connecting to github.com (github.com)|140.82.112.4|:443... connected. HTTP request sent, awaiting response... 302 Found Location: https://codeload.github.com/bitcoin-core/guix.sigs/tar.gz/main [following] --2026-03-01 13:06:22-- https://codeload.github.com/bitcoin-core/guix.sigs/tar.gz/main Resolving codeload.github.com (codeload.github.com)... 140.82.112.9 Connecting to codeload.github.com (codeload.github.com)|140.82.112.9|:443... connected. HTTP request sent, awaiting response... 200 OK Length: unspecified [application/x-gzip] Saving to: ‘master.tar.gz’ master.tar.gz [ <=> ] 1.89M 9.27MB/s in 0.2s 2026-03-01 13:06:22 (9.27 MB/s) - ‘master.tar.gz’ saved [1979851] Now, unpack the archive using the tar -xzvf command: taylor@debian:~$ tar -xzvf master.tar.gz Now we will run two commands: sudo apt install gnupg and gpg --import guix.sigs-main/builder-keys/*. The first command installs a package called GPG that does all the math for us verifying the signatures using public-private key cryptography. The second uses that package to read all the keys that could potentially sign a Bitcoin version. The output should look something like this: taylor@debian:~$ sudo apt install gnupg taylor@debian:~$ gpg --import guix.sigs-main/builder-keys/* gpg: key 188CBB2648416AD5: 6 signatures not checked due to missing keys gpg: /home/taylor/.gnupg/trustdb.gpg: trustdb created gpg: key 188CBB2648416AD5: public key ".0xB10C <b10c@b10c.me>" imported gpg: key 17565732E08E5E41: public key "Ava Chow <me@achow101.com>" imported gpg: key D7CC770B81FD22A8: 2 signatures not checked due to missing keys gpg: key D7CC770B81FD22A8: public key "Ben Carman <benthecarman@live.com>" imported gpg: key 1C2491FFEB0EF770: 2 signatures not checked due to missing keys gpg: key 1C2491FFEB0EF770: public key "Cory Fields <cfields@bitcoinfoundation.org>" imported gpg: key A5E0907A0380E6C3: public key "CoinForensics (SigningKey) <59567284+coinforensics@users.noreply.github.com>" imported gpg: key E13FC145CD3F4304: 15 signatures not checked due to missing keys gpg: key E13FC145CD3F4304: public key "Antoine Poinsot <darosior@protonmail.com>" imported gpg: key 10F2A3D26839321E: public key "davidgumberg <davidzgumberg@gmail.com>" imported gpg: key C37B1C1D44C786EE: public key "Duncan Dean <duncangleeddean@gmail.com>" imported gpg: key 2EBB056FD847F8A7: 12 signatures not checked due to missing keys gpg: key 2EBB056FD847F8A7: public key "Stephan Oeste (it) <it@oeste.de>" imported gpg:
