that we can check whether a developer has signed something, let’s check! Using the gpg --verify command will check any file against the list of keys we have imported. Run this on the signature file with gpg --verify SHA256SUMS.asc: taylor@debian:~$ gpg --verify SHA256SUMS.asc gpg: assuming signed data in 'SHA256SUMS' gpg: Signature made Thu 03 Oct 2024 12:25:02 PM EDT gpg: using RSA key 101598DC823C1B5F9A6624ABA5E0907A0380E6C3 gpg: Good signature from "CoinForensics (SigningKey) <59567284+coinforensics@users.noreply.github.com>" [expired] gpg: Note: This key has expired! Primary key fingerprint: 1015 98DC 823C 1B5F 9A66 24AB A5E0 907A 0380 E6C3 ... [a long list of signatures] ... The critical line in each entry is the gpg: Good signature... line. After that, there are warnings that the key is not certified with a trusted signature. This just means that I have not signed these newly imported keys with my own key. This warning can be safely ignored (per the Core developers: https://bitcoincore.org/en/download/). Finally, check the bitcoin core download itself. taylor@debian:~$ sha256sum --check SHA256SUMS --ignore-missing bitcoin-28.0-x86_64-linux-gnu.tar.gz: OK To recap, we downloaded Bitcoin Core v28.0, a list of hashes for all v28.0 files, a signature file which proves the hashes are authentic, and the keys for all Bitcoin Core signers which proves the signature file is authentic. In order for an attacker to tamper with this download, they would have to compromise the public GitHub listing of all the developers keys, replace all the keys with fake keys, create a modified version of the release, hash it, sign the hashes with the fake keys, then somehow get the fake versions onto the Bitcoin Core file server. Doing any one of those things would raise an army of red flags, so I think we’re safe. Now let’s unpack the compressed file and move the executable binaries into our executable directory: taylor@debian:~$ ls bitcoin-28.0-x86_64-linux-gnu.tar.gz guix.sigs-main master.tar.gz SHA256SUMS SHA256SUMS.asc taylor@debian:~$ tar -xzf bitcoin-28.0-x86_64-linux-gnu.tar.gz taylor@debian:~$ ls bitcoin-28.0 bitcoin-28.0-x86_64-linux-gnu.tar.gz guix.sigs-main master.tar.gz SHA256SUMS SHA256SUMS.asc taylor@debian:~$ sudo cp bitcoin-28.0/bin/* /usr/local/bin [sudo] password for taylor: Now that “bitcoin” is in our executable directory, we should be able to invoke it directly: taylor@debian:~$ bitcoind --version Bitcoin Core version v28.0.0 Copyright (C) 2009-2024 The Bitcoin Core developers Please contribute if you find Bitcoin Core useful. Visit <https://bitcoincore.org/> for further information about the software. The source code is available from <https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin>. This is experimental software. Distributed under the MIT software license, see the accompanying file COPYING or <https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT> taylor@debian:~$ bitcoin-cli --version Bitcoin Core RPC client version v28.0.0 Copyright (C) 2009-2024 The Bitcoin Core developers Please contribute if you find Bitcoin Core useful. Visit <https://bitcoincore.org/> for further information about the software. The source code is available from <https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin>. This is experimental software. Distributed under the MIT software license, see the accompanying file COPYING or <https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT> Next, we need to create a system user to grant file permissions and operate the application. We will create a bitcoin user and give it access to the whole /mnt/bitcoin directory. Use the series of commands below: taylor@debian:~$ sudo useradd -r -m -s /bin/false bitcoin taylor@debian:~$ sudo chown -R bitcoin:bitcoin /mnt/bitcoin taylor@debian:~$ sudo mkdir -p /mnt/bitcoin/.bitcoin taylor@debian:~$ sudo chown bitcoin:bitcoin /mnt/bitcoin/.bitcoin taylor@debian:~$ sudo chmod 750 /mnt/bitcoin/.bitcoin Next, we need to configure how the app should run. Bitcoin is managed by a file called bitcoin.conf. Use the command sudo -u bitcoin nano /mnt/bitcoin/.bitcoin/bitcoin.conf to create this file and open a text editor so we can update it. Use the following values to start: datadir=/mnt/bitcoin listen=1 port=8333 rpcuser=bitcoinrpc rpcpassword=YOUR_GOOD_PASSWORD_HERE rpcbind=0.0.0.0 rpcallowip=127.0.0.1/32 rpcallowip=192.168.1.0/24 Then, make it so only our bitcoin user can read this file with sudo chmod 600 /mnt/bitcoin/.bitcoin/bitcoin.conf: taylor@debian:~$ sudo chmod 600 /mnt/bitcoin/.bitcoin/bitcoin.conf Next, we need to create a systemd service so our Bitcoin core application will automatically run and with the right permission and configuration. sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/bitcoind.service: Add the following to the file, then save and exit (Ctrl + X then Y): [Unit] Description=Bitcoin daemon After=network.target [Service] ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/bitcoind -conf=/mnt/bitcoin/.bitcoin/bitcoin.conf -datadir=/mnt/bitcoin User=bitcoin Group=bitcoin Type=simple Restart=on-failure RestartSec=30 TimeoutStartSec=infinity TimeoutStopSec=600 # Security hardening NoNewPrivileges=true ProtectSystem=strict ProtectHome=true ReadWritePaths=/mnt/bitcoin [Install] WantedBy=multi-user.target Once the service file exists, we need to enable and start the service. Use the systemctl commands below: taylor@debian:~$ sudo systemctl daemon-reload taylor@debian:~$ sudo systemctl enable bitcoind Created symlink '/etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/bitcoind.service' → '/etc/systemd/system/bitcoind.service'. taylor@debian:~$ sudo systemctl start bitcoind To monitor the node’s progress, you can use sudo journalctl -u bitcoind -f. This will let you watch as new logs are generated by the bitcoin node. taylor@debian:~$ sudo journalctl -u bitcoind -f Mar 01 14:11:02 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:02Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 2000 (~0.22%) Mar 01 14:11:03 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:03Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 6000 (~0.67%) Mar 01 14:11:03 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:03Z Loading addresses from DNS seed dnsseed.bitcoin.dashjr-list-of-p2p-nodes.us. Mar 01 14:11:03 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:03Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 10000 (~1.11%) Mar 01 14:11:03 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:03Z New outbound-full-relay v1 peer connected: version: 70016, blocks=938903, peer=3 Mar 01 14:11:03 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:03Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 14000 (~1.56%) Mar 01 14:11:03 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:03Z Loading addresses from DNS seed dnsseed.emzy.de. Mar 01 14:11:04 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:04Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 18000 (~2.01%) Mar 01 14:11:04 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:04Z New outbound-full-relay v1 peer connected: version: 70016, blocks=938903, peer=5 Mar 01 14:11:04 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:04Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 22000 (~2.47%) Mar 01 14:11:04 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:04Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 26000 (~2.94%) Mar 01 14:11:04 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:04Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 30000 (~3.40%) Mar 01 14:11:05 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:05Z Loading addresses from DNS seed seed.bitcoin.sipa.be. Mar 01 14:11:05 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:05Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 34000 (~3.85%) Mar 01 14:11:05 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:05Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 38000 (~4.30%) Mar 01 14:11:05 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:05Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 42000 (~4.75%) Mar 01 14:11:06 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:06Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 44000 (~4.97%) Mar 01 14:11:06 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:06Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 48000 (~5.42%) Mar 01 14:11:06 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:06Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 52000 (~5.87%) Mar 01 14:11:06 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:06Z Loading addresses from DNS seed seed.bitcoin.wiz.biz. Mar 01 14:11:07 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:07Z Loading addresses from DNS seed dnsseed.bluematt.me. Mar 01 14:11:07 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:07Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 56000 (~6.32%) Mar 01 14:11:07 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:07Z Loading addresses from DNS seed seed.btc.petertodd.net. Mar 01 14:11:07 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:07Z Pre-synchronizing blockheaders, height: 60000 (~6.76%) Mar 01 14:11:07 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:07Z 286 addresses found from DNS seeds Mar 01 14:11:07 debian bitcoind[1849]: 2026-03-01T19:11:07Z dnsseed thread exit ... We can see that the node is now syncing blocks and pulling down bootstrap nodes from the DNS seeds run by Core maintainers. Next, we need to make sure we can communicate with the node via RPC. RPC is the protocol used by Bitcoin software to get needed information from nodes. First, let’s try and make an RPC request via the CLI on our debian machine. taylor@debian:~$ sudo -u bitcoin /usr/local/bin/bitcoin-cli -conf=/mnt/bitcoin/.bitcoin/bitcoin.conf getblockchaininfo { "chain": "main", "blocks": 351230, "headers": 938905, "bestblockhash": "00000000000000000b43ad0ef8af67562bd1542ac9ff05bbca28ae63fa160c79", "difficulty": 49446390688.24144, "time": 1428486478, "mediantime": 1428479813, "verificationprogress": 0.04676222132915255, "initialblockdownload": true, "chainwork": "00000000000000000000000000000000000000000005fd5cac8836f0e81c3cde", "size_on_disk": 38695278459, "pruned": false, "warnings": [ ] } Great, it’s working. Now we can try from another machine on our LAN network. C:\Users\taylor>curl -u bitcoinrpc -d "{\"jsonrpc\":\"1.0\",\"id\":\"test\",\"method\":\"getblockchaininfo\",\"params\":[]}" -H "Content-Type: text/plain;" http://192.168.1.252:8332/ Enter host password for user 'bitcoinrpc': {"result":{"chain":"main","blocks":367361,"headers":938906,"bestblockhash":"00000000000000000af51bc19e872b2202f44b834575e52fc03c0cb6403fa458","difficulty":52278304845.59168,"time":1438094261,"mediantime":1438091233,"verificationprogress":0.05612806988650505,"initialblockdownload":true,"chainwork":"00000000000000000000000000000000000000000008cc12c549a1c4fb7d90ba","size_on_disk":47074367637,"pruned":false,"warnings":[]},"error":null,"id":"test"} This confirms that RPC is working as expected and we can communicate with the node over LAN. At this point, we’re done! Depending on how many resources your VM has, the speed of the storage drive you used, and your network bandwidth, syncing the blockchain can take 24 hours to just over a week. Conclusion I took my need for a Bitcoin node as an opportunity to explore TrueNAS’ virtualization features, and so far it’s working out great. If you rely on Bitcoin, please take the time to read many sources and get truly comfortable with your node. This content is for educational/documentary purposes only. I take no responsibility for the consequences of following, or not following, these instructions. Nothing I say constitutes financial advice or the opinions of any former, current, or future employer.

title: Opencode + MapleAI
content: I’ve been playing with Maple, but if an AI tool wants to compete with frontier companies for software development, it must offer a way to avoid the copy-paste game. Keeping AI context up-to-date while I code is an annoying time sink. It’s easier to keep the architecture in my head and tell a bot to re-read a file than to keep every file open in the chat, copying and pasting back and forth. Opencode is an open source coding agent with harnesses for a shocking number of platforms, but Maple is not on the list. Thankfully, the folks at Maple are working on that, and in the meantime we can use their fork to make opencode run on Maple, with any model Maple offers. After tinkering to get Maple’s proxy service into opencode as a provider, I realized the Maple team had already done the work. Step 1: Clone $ git clone https://github.com/marksftw/opencode.git Cloning into 'opencode'... remote: Enumerating objects: 141217, done. remote: Total 141217 (delta 0), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 141217 (from 1) Receiving objects: 100% (141217/141217), 200.81 MiB | 31.11 MiB/s, done. Resolving deltas: 100% (98914/98914), done. Updating files: 100% (2923/2923), done. $ cd ./opencode $ git checkout feature/add-maple-ai-provider Updating files: 100% (3145/3145), done. Switched to a new branch 'feature/add-maple-ai-provider' branch 'feature/add-maple-ai-provider' set up to track 'origin/feature/add-maple-ai-provider'. Step 2: Build $ bun install bun install v1.3.11 (af24e281) ... + @actions/artifact@5.0.1 + @tsconfig/bun@1.0.9 + @types/mime-types@3.0.1 + @typescript/native-preview@7.0.0-dev.20251207.1 + glob@13.0.5 + husky@9.1.7 + oxlint@1.60.0 + oxlint-tsgolint@0.21.0 + prettier@3.6.2 + semver@7.7.4 + sst@3.18.10 + turbo@2.8.13 + @aws-sdk/client-s3@3.933.0 + heap-snapshot-toolkit@1.1.3 + typescript@5.8.2 (v6.0.3 available) 4737 packages installed [187.33s] Step 3: Run the Proxy If we don’t run the proxy, Maple won’t even show up as an option in the model providers list. # first, download the proxy image $ docker pull ghcr.io/opensecretcloud/maple-proxy:latest latest: Pulling from opensecretcloud/maple-proxy ff86ea2e5edc: Pull complete 690ad8afb7ef: Pull complete 6c668459f6b7: Pull complete 26580263d70d: Pull complete 17fb03652f5e: Pull complete 0b912093bb1a: Pull complete Digest: sha256:2efaba73ed9876f9772bfbd0847fe8772746a7d25daf04bb1467dfaf0b1033b4 Status: Downloaded newer image for ghcr.io/opensecretcloud/maple-proxy:latest ghcr.io/opensecretcloud/maple-proxy:latest # second, run it $ docker run -p 8080:8080 -e MAPLE_API_KEY=${MAPLE_API_KEY} ghcr.io/opensecretcloud/maple-proxy:latest error during connect: Post "http://%2F%2F.%2Fpipe%2FdockerDesktopLinuxEngine/v1.49/images/create?fromImage=ghcr.io%2Fopensecretcloud%2Fmaple-proxy&tag=latest": open //./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine: The system cannot find the file specified. 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296040Z INFO maple_proxy: Starting Maple Proxy Server 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296104Z INFO maple_proxy: Version: 0.1.8 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296109Z INFO maple_proxy: Backend URL: https://enclave.trymaple.ai 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296132Z INFO maple_proxy: Binding to: 0.0.0.0:8080 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296137Z INFO maple_proxy: No default API key - clients must provide Authorization header 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296247Z INFO maple_proxy: CORS enabled for all origins 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296302Z INFO maple_proxy: 🚀 Maple Proxy Server started successfully! 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296306Z INFO maple_proxy: 📋 Available endpoints: 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296309Z INFO maple_proxy: GET /health - Health check 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296310Z INFO maple_proxy: GET /v1/models - List available models 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296312Z INFO maple_proxy: POST /v1/chat/completions - Create chat completions (streaming & non-streaming) 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296315Z INFO maple_proxy: 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296319Z INFO maple_proxy: 💡 Usage: 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296323Z INFO maple_proxy: Set MAPLE_API_KEY environment variable or provide Authorization: Bearer <key> header 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296329Z INFO maple_proxy: Compatible with any OpenAI client library! 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296332Z INFO maple_proxy: 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296335Z INFO maple_proxy: 🔗 Example curl: 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296338Z INFO maple_proxy: curl http://0.0.0.0:8080 \ 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296342Z INFO maple_proxy: -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_MAPLE_API_KEY" \ 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296346Z INFO maple_proxy: -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296351Z INFO maple_proxy: -d '{"model": "gpt-4", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello!"}]}' 2026-05-07T22:49:48.296356Z INFO maple_proxy: /v1/chat/completions Step 4: Run Opencode First, we must give opencode our API token so the Maple proxy can handle our requests. $ bun run dev auth login > dev > bun run --cwd packages/opencode --conditions=browser src/index.ts auth login T Add credential | o Select provider | Maple AI | • Maple AI is a TEE-based private AI provider. | | Setup: | 1. Start the Maple Proxy (desktop app or Docker) | 2. Generate an API key in the Maple app | 3. Enter your API key below | | The default proxy URL is http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1 | To use a different URL, add to opencode.json: | { "provider": { "maple": { "options": { "baseURL": "http://your-url/v1" } } } } | o Enter your API key | •••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••• | — Done Anything that comes after bun run dev will be passed in as a command line argument. So bun run dev auth login gets parsed as opencode auth login, which is the proper command to trigger the login mechanism. Since opencode /directory/ is the correct syntax to run opencode in a specific folder, we’ll use that when running the full program: $ bun run dev ~/opencode-test > dev > bun run --cwd packages/opencode --conditions=browser src/index.ts ~/opencode-test After a brief build period, you should see a new terminal interface with the desired directory in the bottom left and one of Maple’s models already selected (mine was Kimi k2.6): If you see the default Big Pickle Opencode Zen, that suggests the Maple proxy is not running. Let’s ask Kimi a basic question to make sure everything is working: Great, now let’s see if it can instruct opencode to interact with the file system: Success! Step 5: Evaporate your Tokens Using these tools consumes significantly more tokens than a chat session, especially with lots of files, models that spawn agents, or models that do lots of reasoning. Whether the cost tradeoff makes sense is based entirely on what your time is worth. Happy hacking!

title: Workshop Companion Instructions
content: This article contains step-by-step instructions, with screenshots, for creating a GitHub Pages personal portfolio site. Exact instructions for the initial set up and configuration of two popular themes, Al Folio, Chirpy, and Hydejack, are included. Thanks to NC State’s College of Engineering, Tiva Rocco, and Dr. Sarah Heckman for helping make this workshop happen! Selecting a Theme The number of themes available for Jekyll sites is essentially unlimited. To help you learn the ropes of GitHub Pages and Jekyll, we are offering expertise in two themes: Chirpy and Al Folio. For a blog style website, choose the Chirpy theme: https://chirpy.cotes.page/ For a portfolio style website, choose the Al Folio theme: https://alshedivat.github.io/al-folio/ Follow the links to view sample versions of each site and pick the one you like the best. If it’s not perfect, that’s OK! The skills you learn in this workshop can be applied to another theme later. Al Folio First, navigate to https://github.com/alshedivat/al-folio. In the top-right corner of the repository, click Use this template > Create a new repository. Name the repository username.github.io. Make sure it is Public and click Create repository. For Al Folio to work, we must grant permissions to GitHub Actions. In your repository, go to Settings > Actions > General. Toward the bottom, update the Workflow permissions and click Save. Next, navigate to _config.yml in your repository and click edit. Update the url to https://<username>.github.ioand baseurl to "" as shown below. Click Commit changes... in the top-right corner and save your changes. After your website is built (you can check this in the Actions tab), confirm that a new branch has been created in the repository called gh-pages. We need to tell GitHub Pages to use this new branch. In your repository, click Settings and go to Pages in the left-hand sidebar. Ensure Source is set to Deploy from a branch. Under the branch selector, make sure gh-pages is selected and click Save. Next, let’s confirm we can edit and rebuild the site with our changes. Navigate to _config.yml in your repository and click edit. Update the title and name attributes as shown below. Click Commit changes... in the top-right corner and save your changes. After the website rebuilds, refresh and confirm your changes took effect. Success! Now, the real works begins. Continue making changes to the configuration according to your goals for the site. The default pages contain instructions for making changes, plus samples of how to create blog posts, projects, and more. For an easy working environment, try out GitHub Codespaces (instructions below in GitHub Codespaces)! Chirpy Navigate to https://github.com/cotes2020/chirpy-starter. In the top-right corner of the repository, click Use this template > Create a new repository. Name the repository username.github.io. Make sure it is Public and click Create repository. Now that we have a repository, we need to enable GitHub Pages. In your repository, click Settings and go to Pages in the left-hand sidebar. Ensure Source is set to Deploy from a branch. Under the branch selector, make sure main is selected and click Save. Navigate to _config.yml in your repository and click edit. Change the url to username.github.io. Click Commit changes... in the top-right corner and save your changes. After your website is built (you can check this in the Actions tab), navigate to https://username.github.io. You should see the default Chirpy website. Now, we can start configuring our website. First, let’s change the name and tagline. Navigate to _config.yml and click edit. Change the title and tagline attributes to whatever you want! Click Commit changes... in the top-right corner and save your changes. Once the website is rebuilt, reload the site and you see your changes. Your website is running! Now, the real work begins. Use _config.yml to adjust the other aspects of the website as you see fit. Write new articles in .md (markdown) format and upload them to the _posts folder. For long-term work, I recommend following the Chirpy developers’ instructions to set up a development environment on your local machine, found here https://chirpy.cotes.page/posts/getting-started/. The developers’ have also written instructions to create a new article, found here https://chirpy.cotes.page/posts/write-a-new-post/. For an easy working environment, try out GitHub Codespaces (instructions below in GitHub Codespaces)! GitHub Codespaces Instead of configuring a local machine to work on your website, we can use GitHub Codespaces to do our work in the cloud! This should be done after you have successfully built a working version of the website. To open a codespace, click Code in your repository and choose the Codespaces tab. Then, click to open a codespace on main. Here, I’m using the Al Folio example page. After loading into my codespace, you may notice it looks a lot like VSCode. We’re basically running VSCode on GitHub’s servers and accessing it via a web browser. After opening the codespace, you may notice some terminal activity as Jekyll automatically starts and builds this temporary version of our website. Once the build is finished, you will see a prompt to open the website in the bottom right corner. If you open this side-by-side with your codespace, you can make changes and watch them happen on your site in real time. This is much faster than making changes in the repository and waiting for the site to be rebuilt in GitHub Pages every time! For example, I can open the _config.yml file in my editor and change the name to John Smith. Jekyll will automatically rebuild the whole site after I save my changes, and after the rebuild is finished, the website will reload with my changes: Once you feel ready to deploy your changes on your public site, you can use the codespace source control to commit directly to your repository. Once the changes are built in your public site, refresh and check out your changes! GitHub Codespaces might behave differently depending on your theme. Some themes require that you stop (ctrl + c on the terminal) and restart the website to see your changes. This is still faster than waiting for GitHub Actions to rebuild your public site. Keep the following commands handy: $ bundle install $ bundle exec jekyll serve $ bundle exec jekyll clean

title: Custom Domain, Bot Protection, and Region Locks on GitHub Pages with Cloudflare
content: The Benefits of Custom Domains A custom domain has many benefits. Domains are the foundation layer of web browsing, and owning one gives you significant control over your digital life. Because domains are what humans keep in their brains and what computers use to find content, owning a custom domain makes your whole digital life portable between providers. I currently host my webpage at GitHub, but if GitHub exploded tomorrow, I could point wtthomas.org at a thousand other hosts, and visitors would click on the same links they always have without noticing a difference. If my email provider goes bankrupt and terminates my service, I can point my custom domain at a new email provider and continue to receive all my email. The remainder of this article will assume that you have a custom domain hosted/registered with Cloudflare. There are significant privacy risks when registering a custom domain. Make sure you understand the WHOIS protection offered by your registrar. Mistakes here cannot be corrected later. Pointing a Custom Domain at GitHub Pages First, you need to confirm to GitHub that you own the domain you claim to own. In the GitHub settings under Pages, click Add a domain. GitHub will provide a TXT record which you must add to Cloudflare. GitHub will execute DNS queries against your domain and check for the presence of this record, which will confirm that you control the domain. Go to Cloudflare > Your Custom Domain > DNS > Records. Add the TXT record with the token GitHub provided. Once this is done, go back to GitHub and click verify. This typically happens instantly, but it can take up to 24 hours for your DNS changes to propagate. Next, go to the GitHub repository which contains your webpage. Under Settings > Code and automation > Pages, you can configure the custom domain to point at this repository. GitHub Pages are very versatile, and you should configure the deployment settings according to your set up. My environment uses GitHub Actions to build the raw site HTML from a private repository, so my source is the root of the main branch in the Pages repository, but most environments are simpler and just pull from GitHub Actions directly. Choose what works best for you. Next, add Page’s IP addresses to your Cloudflare DNS. At the time of writing, the IPs that GitHub uses are as follows: 185.199.108.153 185.199.109.153 185.199.110.153 185.199.111.153 See GitHub’s Docs for the most up-to-date IP addresses and DNS record requirements. We will create one A record in DNS for each of these IPs. You can configure them as proxied by flipping the proxy toggle. This will allow us to run HTTPS in the next step. Finally, go to SSL/TLS under your custom domain and select Full . This will allow visitors to connect using HTTPS handled entirely by Cloudflare and GitHub. Bot and Scraping Protection The meteoric rise of LLM-based AI tools opened the floodgates of data scraping and website crawlers. As large sites like Reddit fight scrapers, the scrapers become hungrier to hoover up as much data as possible before it’s too late. While my humble website is no Library of Alexandria, I strongly prefer not to have my content scraped by AI crawlers or archived and published by third party websites. I am not so naïve as to think this Cloudflare feature is a silver bullet. My site will still be scraped, my posts will be cataloged and archived against my wishes, but that is the price to pay for a public presence. I am happy to take Cloudflare’s side in the perpetual war against unauthorized access, and I am grateful for the victories that occur on the margins. Cloudflare offers several types and tiers of bot management, but their free option is sufficient for the scope of a page like mine. Start by going to Security > Overview and looking for the Bot traffic section. Under settings, you’ll see your options to turn on various free Cloudflare tools. I turn all of these on, and I’ll show you some sample success blocks later. The AI Labyrinth is a unique Cloudflare invention which sends AI crawlers down an infinite maze of undesirable content, burning time and money for companies that don’t respect the site owner’s choices. Given this incentive to respect your choices, suddenly bots become more compliant. If I click on my AI Crawl Control tab, I can see successful blocks of bots from various companies over time. Bot fight mode focuses more on keeping non-AI bots away from your page. These bots typically aren’t interested in harvesting content, they’re checking if your site is out-of-date and vulnerable to known attacks. I’ve included a sample below of some of the requests Cloudflare blocked. These bots were targeting wordpress endpoints that have known flaws which, if unpatched, might allow an attacker to gain unauthorized access to your infrastructure. With GitHub pages, this isn’t nearly as much of a concern, but I would rather have these criminals kept away from my site anyway. I expect this field to rapidly advance, and I encourage you to stay current with changes on Cloudflare’s capabilities and recommendations. See Cloudflare’s Docs for up-to-date instructions or best practices. Region Locks Security admins from all walks of work will tell you that blanket region blocks are a great place to start. While an attacker or scraper can get around this with a VPN or a VPS, we want to force them to take this extra step. Never be the lowest hanging fruit or the most attractive target. In my case, I chose to whitelist traffic from the United States. My target audience is colleagues in my field, potential employers, and fellow students at my university. All of the traffic to my site should be coming from the USA. To implement this in Cloudflare, go to Settings > Security rules. If you followed the steps above regarding the bot protections, you may see a rule already configured here for blocking bots and AI agents. Click Create rule > Custom rules. My USA-Only rule configuration is shown below, feel free to modify to fit your preferences. These custom rules are very powerful, so make sure you test thoroughly once implemented. See Cloudflare’s Docs for up-to-date instructions or best practices.

title: 27 Million Read Errors
content: I recently responded to a data-threatening disk failure on my NAS, this article catalogues my response. I take no responsibility for data loss as a result of repeating the contents of this article. Your hardware and software configuration differ from mine. Always consult up-to-date documentation from hardware and software providers. Backups are king! Failure Notification This may be the most important part of this article. If your system has no way to push information to you, or to a place where you see it, you will probably discover a failure too late. TrueNAS offers several methods to push system notifications, I use two: Telegram and Email. I will briefly cover establishing both. TrueNAS Email For Email alerts to work, you must first configure a Send method in the System > General Settings. TrueNAS offers SMTP or Gmail OAuth. Follow your email provider’s instructions to configure this, and send a test email to confirm it works. Once you have established successful email delivery, go to System > Alert Settings. Click Add and set your desired receiving email address for alerts. As discussed later, I configure my email to use the Notice alert level. TrueNAS Telegram Telegram has a robust ecosystem for bots, but creating and configuring a bot can be obtuse. You will need a token and a chat ID . To get a token, you must create a bot. Telegram has an automated tool for this called BotFather. DM BotFather and use the /start command to get started. After you follow the prompts, he will spit out a token. Keep this token secure. To get a Chat ID, you first need to decide who the bot should talk to. If you only want your notifications to come to your Telegram account, you can create a DM chat with the bot and supply that Chat ID. If you want multiple people to receive notifications (or if you want all your bots to send to the same place, if you have more than one bot), you can create a group chat and use that instead. To acquire a chat ID for a one-on-one DM, first send your bot a message. You can click on the bot’s username in the BotFather DM to start that conversation. Send a message like “hello”. Next, use Telegram’s API to get the chat ID. Open a web browser and navigate to https://api.telegram.org/bot(BOT_TOKEN_HERE)/getUpdates. Replace (BOT_TOKEN_HERE) with your bot token, no parentheses. You will see an output that contains the chat ID, similar to below. If the result JSON object is empty, send the bot another message and try again. To acquire a chat ID for a group chat, you’ll use the same steps as above, but you must create the group chat and give the bot adequate permissions before sending a message to the group chat. To add members, click on the group chat and click the plus icon. Search for the bot’s username and add it. If you send a message and don’t get a useful API result, consider right-clicking on the bot’s name in the group chat members list and clicking Promote to Admin. Once again, we are searching for the chat ID object in the JSON response. Now that you have a Chat ID and Bot Token, we can set it up in TrueNAS. Go to System > Alert Settings and click Add in the Alert Services section. Under Type, select Telegram. Fill in the rest of the information. I advise starting with the Level set to Info. This may result in more alerts than you want, but it gives you the opportunity to refine TrueNAS’s alert levels system-wide. I will not cover this here, but TrueNAS offers granular alert controls. I personally leave my Email option on Notice and my Telegram bot on Info, without changing the default attributions for events. Configure the Alert Settings appropriately for your software, hardware, and risk tolerance. Once everything is configured, send a test alert to confirm your configuration works. If not, review the steps above and consult Telegram’s documentation. My Alert Below is the Email alert that got my attention. Uh oh. Not good. Even though the pool is still online (as opposed to “degraded” or even worse, “offline”), unrecoverable error sounds pretty nasty. An additional piece of context is necessary: these drives are super old. Old drives plus any stability warning is a recipe for immediate intervention. After handling the situation I put the trouble drive on my test bench and found that it has logged 55,000 power-on hours. That’s more than six years of continuous operation. It had a good run. Investigation Step one, log in to the TrueNAS dashboard and see what we can see. I see a red X next to my pool’s ZFS Health, that’s not ideal. I need more information, time to head to the shell. System > Shell. # Let's do a broad system sweep for errors. $ sudo dmesg | grep -i error ... a handful of errors about my ARCHIVE_POOL ... # Let's see if the system journal has anything to say. $ sudo journalctl -xe | grep -i 'kernel' | grep -i 'error' ...
